atoms.semantic_claim: The old engine launcher creates a synchronous backfill blocking handler from the transaction pool and provider, passes it into pipeline construction, and uses it to process canonical-state scheduled/async transaction work before asynchronous chain notifications proceed.

destinations.evidence: Current engine launch does not construct create_backfill_blocking_handler, does not pass a transaction-pool backfill handler into build_networked_pipeline, and exact searches found no process_canonical_state_transactions callback in this launch path.
Current pipeline setup has no blocking_handler parameter and no ExecutionStage::with_blocking_handler call, so the old execution-stage synchronous registration path is absent.

Current pool maintenance now registers pool.on_blocking_canonical_state_change through provider.subscribe_blocking_canon_state before spawning the existing async maintain_transaction_pool_future. This preserves the blocking-before-notifications ordering for Ritual async lifecycle side effects while keeping ordinary txpool maintenance on the v2 stream path.

The maintenance task still handles Commit/Reorg notifications and calls pool.on_canonical_state_change(update), preserving ordinary v2 txpool maintenance. The ordering-sensitive Ritual async lifecycle side effects are also exposed through TransactionPoolExt::on_blocking_canonical_state_change for the provider-level blocking subscription.

Pool::process_blocking_canonical_state_change processes async commitments, async settlements, and expiry cleanup from the canonical tip. Pool::on_canonical_state_change delegates to the same idempotent hook after ordinary txpool updates, so existing stream maintenance remains safe while the provider blocking subscriber restores pre-notification visibility.

atoms.equivalence_argument: Resolved with v2-style wiring. The port still does not restore the old engine-launch/pipeline blocking-handler plumbing, but it now registers the transaction-pool canonical async lifecycle hook through provider.subscribe_blocking_canon_state. CanonicalInMemoryState runs blocking subscribers before async canonical broadcasts, so async metadata/sender-lock/settlement cleanup state is visible before downstream notification consumers can race result submission.
atoms.negative_test_candidate: Keep the txpool regression that processes a canonical async commitment through process_blocking_canonical_state_change and proves metadata plus sender lock are populated before stream maintenance.
atoms.notes: Reviewed and updated 2026-05-11: resolved by provider-level blocking subscription in spawn_pool_maintenance_task plus TransactionPoolExt::on_blocking_canonical_state_change. The v2 path preserves ordering without restoring execution-stage plumbing.

atoms.semantic_claim: The old engine launcher passes the shared launch-context SPC verifier into `AddOnsContext` so engine validators/add-ons can access the same verifier instance.

destinations.evidence: Current Ethereum add-ons build an ExecutorRegistry with capacity 100, construct SpcVerifier::new_from_env_simple(...)? with the node provider, and pass the verifier into ritual_async_rpc::module. The ? means verifier construction failure aborts add-on launch instead of allowing the async-result RPC without verifier state.

The async-result RPC loads the canonical AsyncJobTracker job, rebuilds the SPC request from canonical job context, syncs the verifier registry from state at the commit block, calls spc_verifier.verify_single, and only then calls pool.submit_async_result. Missing registry/job data, invalid requests, invalid proofs, and verifier errors all return failed async responses before pool state is advanced.

Block verification still includes the SPC phase: scheduled verification, scheduled censorship, async commitments, settlements, verify_spc_calls, nonce locks, and sequencing rights all run from parent state. This covers verifier-side block acceptance without needing an AddOnsContext-held SpcVerifier object.

atoms.equivalence_argument: Confirmed after mechanical review: AddOnsContext no longer receives a launch-context verifier, but active SPC verifier behavior is installed in ethereum/node RPC setup and block verification. This is a non-exact ownership/API change, not a missing verifier FSM transition.
atoms.negative_test_candidate: Keep RPC verifier fail-closed tests around ritual_submitAsyncResult and invalid SPC proof/job/registry cases.
atoms.notes: Reviewed 2026-05-11: verifier handoff is structurally covered elsewhere; no unresolved proof remains for atom 2.

summary: Reviewed and resolved: atom 1 is now structurally covered by provider-level blocking canonical-state subscription from txpool maintenance, preserving the old blocking-before-async-notification ordering without restoring execution-stage plumbing. Atom 2 remains structurally covered by current ethereum/node RPC setup and block verification.
notes: No create_backfill_blocking_handler or process_canonical_state_transactions engine-launch registration exists; this is intentionally not restored in the v2 architecture.
Current pool maintenance registers TransactionPoolExt::on_blocking_canonical_state_change with provider.subscribe_blocking_canon_state.
Pool::process_blocking_canonical_state_change processes async commitments, settlements, and cleanup before async canonical notifications are broadcast.
The focused txpool regression and local HTTP async traffic passed after this wiring.
SPC verifier handoff is covered by ethereum/node RPC-local verifier construction and state-backed block verification.
